_note: GENERATED by tools/gen-expressions.mjs — do not edit by hand. Source: the { he, translit, fr } arrays already shipping in the idiom/slang lessons, joined onto the hand-written usage notes in tools/expressions-curation.json. Regenerate: node tools/gen-expressions.mjs build

categories.label: Street Hebrew
categories.blurb: Arabic loanwords. This is what you actually hear on the street, in the shuk, in a cab. Not one of these is in a textbook, and you cannot follow a normal conversation without them.

categories.label: The Yiddish Layer
categories.blurb: Ashkenazi Yiddish that survived into Israeli Hebrew. Carries affection, sarcasm and complaint — registers Hebrew itself is oddly bad at.

categories.label: Reactions
categories.blurb: What you say back. Fluency is mostly reacting at the right speed, not producing long sentences.

categories.label: Blessings & Wishes
categories.blurb: The ritual layer. Israelis say these constantly, secular ones included — they are social reflexes, not religious statements.

categories.label: Pushback
categories.blurb: Disagreeing without it being a fight. Israeli conversation is more direct than French or English; flat contradiction is normal and not rude.

categories.label: Small Talk & The Interrogation
categories.blurb: The opener, then the famously personal questions. An Israeli will ask your salary and your marital status within ten minutes. It reads as interest, not intrusion.

categories.label: Fillers & Hedges
categories.blurb: The words that buy you thinking time and make you sound like a speaker instead of a translator.

categories.label: Closing a Conversation
categories.blurb: Leaving well. Israelis close warm and vague — the exact goodbye matters less than the warmth.

categories.label: Body Idioms
categories.blurb: Heart, head, hand, mouth. The oldest idiom layer, shared with the Tanakh, still used daily.

categories.label: Animal Idioms
categories.blurb: Mostly transparent, and mostly identical to the English or French ones — free vocabulary.

categories.label: Food Idioms
categories.blurb: Bread, salt, milk and honey. Half biblical, half kitchen.

expressions.usage: The default yes/fine/cool. Answers 'how are you', accepts a plan, closes a deal. Safe with anyone under ~60; do not use with a clerk at Misrad Hapnim.
Let's go / hurry / enough. The single most used word in Israel. Also ends a conversation: 'yalla, bye'. Everyone, everywhere, all day.
Genuine surprise, or 'huh, really'. Tone carries all of it — flat walla is polite acknowledgment, rising walla is real astonishment.
Great / excellent, about a thing: achla place, achla guy. Interchangeable with sababa but rates quality rather than agreement.
Content, pleased — usually said about someone else, often with a nod: 'he's mabsut'. Slightly older-generation.
Mate / bro, between men. Warm, or heavily sarcastic in an argument ('listen, habibi…') — same word, opposite temperature.
Straight, no diplomacy. 'Dugri?' invites the blunt answer. A very Israeli value: you are asking to skip the padding.

expressions.fr: I mean / kinda / so to speak
expressions.usage: I mean / sort of. Hedges a claim or fills a pause. Overusing it is a tell that you learned Hebrew on the street, which is a good problem.
Enough, done, drop it. Sharper than 'day'. Ends an argument, or a kid's tantrum.
A real insult, not banter — Arabic origin, vulgar at the root. Recognize it, do not deploy it.
The all-purpose prod: so? / well? / get on with it. Impatience, encouragement, or an eye-roll depending entirely on the vowel length.
Nerve, gall. Half-condemnation half-admiration — Israelis are quietly proud of chutzpa as a national trait.
Poor thing. Pity with a faint sigh, often at someone's misfortune you are not going to fix. Older speakers, or ironic among young ones.
A pest — someone who will not stop asking. Affectionate about a child, less so about a colleague.
A hapless fool. Mild and almost fond; you would say it about a nephew, not a boss.
The chronically unlucky one. The classic pairing: the shlemiel spills the soup, the shlimazel is who it lands on.
No problem. Accepts a request or waves off a mistake. The reflex answer to 'slicha'.
All good. The default answer to ma nishma, and the default reassurance that nothing is wrong.
Totally fine — an emphatic yes to a proposal. Warmer than plain 'beseder'.
Great / nice one. Genuine to a child or a friend; from a bureaucrat with a flat tone it is pure sarcasm.
Excellent. Neutral enough for work and a teacher, unlike sababa.
Well done — real respect for an achievement. Said to soldiers, to kids, to anyone who just did something hard.
Nice work. Workplace register, on a finished job.
Amazing — the literal reading is the exact opposite of the meaning. Tone decides: enthusiastic = incredible, flat and weary = genuinely a waste of time. The classic oleh trap.
There's nothing like it — top-tier praise for food, a place, a performance.
How fun! Unguarded delight — at plans, at good news. 'Keyf' has no clean English equivalent.
Totally / really! Agreement with force. Also 'mamash lo' = absolutely not.
Really? Plain curiosity. Sharpen the tone and it becomes doubt.
Exactly. Endorses what was just said. Also literal: 'be-diyuk be-shalosh' = at three sharp.
It's fine, don't worry about it. The standard absolution after someone apologizes or breaks something small.
Interesting. Real interest, or the polite parking of something you disagree with — same as in English.
Wow. Borrowed straight from English and used exactly the same way.
You don't say! (m.) — surprise at news. Can be dry sarcasm if you already knew.
I get you (m.). Signals comprehension and sympathy at once. 'Mevina' if you are a woman.
Bottom line / honestly. Cuts to the point, or concedes one: 'tachles, you're right'. Yiddish by way of Hebrew tachlit.

expressions.fr: precisely / on the contrary / on purpose
expressions.usage: Actually / on the contrary / deliberately. Notoriously hard for olim: it flags that reality contradicts the expectation. 'Davka tov' = actually good, despite what you assumed.
Congratulations — for what already happened (birth, wedding, new job). Never a wish for the future, unlike the English 'good luck'.
Good luck — before the exam, the interview, the drive. The forward-looking counterpart to mazal tov.
Thank God. Constant, secular speakers included — it is closer to punctuation than to theology. A normal answer to ma nishma.
God willing — about the future. Religious speakers mean it; secular ones use it as a hedge, like inshallah.
expressions.literal: with the help of the Name

expressions.fr: in due time / at a good hour
expressions.usage: In due time — said to a pregnant woman, or about anything awaited. Superstition-adjacent: you avoid naming the date directly.
No way / since when? Rejects a premise outright. Also the warm refusal of an offer to pay — 'ma pitom, I've got it'.
Not true! Flat contradiction. Normal volume in an Israeli argument; nobody is offended.
I disagree (m.). The neutral, workplace-safe version. 'Maskima' if you are a woman.
You're wrong (m.). Direct and completely acceptable between peers. Would be startling in English, is not here.
Oh come on. Exasperation at something absurd or unfair. One of the most Israeli sounds there is.
Come on already / stop it. Half-annoyed, half-affectionate. Said to a friend, a child, a whole room.
Impossible! Refuses a demand — the other half of the sentence is usually a bureaucrat's.
That can't be. Disbelief at news, not at a person.
No way — refusal or disbelief. Young and emphatic. Also the delighted kind: 'eyn matzav!' at news too good to be true.
What do you care? (m.) Sharp. Pushes back on someone meddling; not for strangers unless you want a fight.
Stop (m.). Blunt imperative — 'tafsiki' to a woman. Fine with friends and children, harsh with strangers.
I'd rather not answer (m.). Formal and final, without insult. Useful with the interrogation questions above.
The standard 'how are you'. Expects 'beseder' or 'hakol beseder', not an actual report on your life.
Slightly more casual than ma nishma. Between friends, or a shopkeeper who knows you.
What's going on. Friendly, a bit breezy — a peer, not a stranger.
What's new — for someone you have not seen in a while. Genuinely invites news, unlike ma nishma.
How's life. Warmer and broader; implies you have time to actually hear the answer.
Are you new here (m.). Prompted by your accent. Not a challenge — the follow-up is usually help.
Why did you make aliyah (m.). Asked sincerely and often, sometimes within minutes. A short honest answer lands better than a speech.

expressions.fr: how long have you been here? (m.)
expressions.usage: How long have you been here (m.). Follows 'where are you from' almost automatically.
Are you married (m.). Asked by near-strangers without a hint of awkwardness. Frequently precedes an offer to introduce you to someone.
Do you have kids (m.). Same register as asking the time. Deflecting is what would seem strange.
How old are you (m.). Normal here. 'Bat kama at?' to a woman — and no, it is not rude.
How much do you earn (m.). Genuinely asked, genuinely answered. If you would rather not: 'ze ishi miday'.

expressions.fr: how much did you pay for the apartment? (m.)
expressions.usage: What did you pay for the apartment (m.). Real-estate prices are a national sport; expect the question and an opinion on your answer.

expressions.fr: what do you think of the government? (m.)
expressions.usage: What do you think of the government (m.). Everyone has a view and will share it. Safe to have none out loud while you are new.
That's too personal. The polite exit from the interrogation. Says it without offending — Israelis take it as a fair move.

expressions.fr: you ask a lot of questions (m.)
expressions.usage: You ask a lot of questions (m.). Said with a smile it is banter, and usually earns respect rather than an apology.
My Hebrew isn't perfect yet. Buys patience instantly. Better than apologizing — it states a fact and keeps the conversation in Hebrew.

Speak slower please (m.). The most useful sentence of your first year. Note the register: a bare imperative is blunt — softening it to "efshar ledaber le'at yoter?" (can you speak slower?) is what most Israelis would actually say to a stranger.
With pleasure / gladly. Accepts a favor request warmly. Also answers ma nishma: 'be-keyf' = chilling, all good.
Enough / stop. Also an intensifier: 'day tov' = pretty good. The parent's word, heard in every playground in the country.
In short / long story short. Opens the summary, or cuts your own rambling before someone else does.
Sort of / in a way. Young slang. 'Be-keta shel' = kind of like.
You know? / get it? (m.) Tag question that checks you are still followed. Sprinkled through speech like the English 'right?'.
Honestly / truly. Adds weight to what follows. Without the question mark, it is emphasis rather than doubt.
I'm not sure (m.). Honest hedge — 'betucha' if you are a woman.
Let me think (m.). Buys a beat without conceding the point. 'Tni li' to a woman.
See you. The standard goodbye when a next meeting is vague but assumed.
We'll talk. Ends a call. Often means nothing specific will be scheduled, and everyone knows it.
That was fun. Said leaving a meal or an evening. Genuine, low-effort warmth.
Take care (m.). 'Tishmeri' to a woman. Carries real weight here — it is not just filler.
Regards to the family. Often shortened to 'd"ash'. Expected once someone knows your family exists.
expressions.literal: a demand of peace to the family

expressions.usage: Have a great day. Slightly formal — a shopkeeper, an email sign-off.
Generous, kind. Usually 'yesh lo lev shel zahav'. Identical to the English.
Unmoved by suffering. Also identical to the English idiom.
Broken heart — romantic loss, or plain grief.
To pay attention / notice. Extremely common and fully literal in use: 'sim lev!' = watch out / notice this.
With all my heart. Sincere and a bit elevated — a speech, a letter, a thank-you that matters.
Takes initiative, does more than asked. High praise — army-born and now everywhere in work culture. Note the trap: it does NOT mean arrogant.
Does the bare minimum, avoids responsibility. The insult that pairs with rosh gadol. A real cultural fault line.
Open-minded, quick. Praise for someone who adapts.
First and foremost. Written and formal-spoken register — news, speeches.
Someone who talks too much or cannot keep a secret. Same as the English.
Gossip — but with religious weight: a genuine sin in halacha, and the term is used seriously even by secular Israelis.
By word of mouth. How a restaurant or a plumber gets found here.
In private, just the two of us. 'Bo nedaber arba eynayim' = let's talk alone. No connection to glasses.
The evil eye. Live superstition, not folklore: praise a baby and someone will add a deflection. Related: 'bli ayin ha-ra'.
Generous. 'Be-yad rechava' = generously, lavishly.
To shake on it — to seal a deal by hand. The handshake is the contract in the shuk.
Hand in hand — together, jointly. Also the name of the shared Arab-Jewish school network.
A listening ear. 'Natan lo ozen keshevet' = heard him out properly.
Cornered, out of options. Same image as the English.
Mortal, human, fallible. Biblical register — used to excuse human limits: 'he's only basar va-dam'.
Stubborn as a donkey. Said about someone who will not budge. 'Chamor' alone is an insult — an idiot.
Works like a donkey (m.) — grinding, thankless work. A complaint, occasionally a boast.
The reliable one who carries the team. Compliment.
Trojan horse. Politics, security and computing alike — same borrowing as everywhere else.

expressions.fr: cat in a sack / pig in a poke
expressions.usage: Buying blind. 'Al tikne chatul ba-sak' = don't buy without looking. The French 'chat en poche', the English 'pig in a poke'.
Cat and mouse — a chase, a game of evasion.
A miserable life. Complaint, usually about work.
Lion-hearted, courageous. 'Yesh lo lev arye'.
Walked into the hostile room. Straight out of Daniel; used for a hard meeting or an interview.
I heard through the grapevine, and I'm not naming the source. Identical to the English.
A tireless worker. Compliment, with a hint of 'and never stops'.
Sly, clever — grudging admiration, not condemnation.
Clumsy, tactless. The bull is an elephant here; the shop is the same.
expressions.literal: an elephant in a china shop

expressions.usage: Forgets nothing — including, usually, a grudge.
Guinea pig, test subject. 'Shafan' alone also means coward.
A big shot, someone who matters. 'Dag katan' is the opposite — a nobody in this room.

expressions.fr: land flowing with milk and honey
expressions.usage: The biblical description of the Land (Exodus 3:8). Quoted sincerely, and just as often with irony at a bus strike.
expressions.literal: a land flowing with milk and honey

expressions.usage: The decent, unglamorous backbone people. Same sense as the English.
Take it with a grain of salt — treat the claim with doubt.
The bare minimum, subsistence. 'Chai al lechem u-mayim' = scraping by.
Reward and punishment — politics, negotiation, parenting.
The same old thing repackaged. From Pirkei Avot; still deployed at every product launch.
expressions.literal: old wine in a new jug

expressions.usage: Treading very carefully around someone. The English walks on eggshells; Hebrew dances.
Destroyed him — in an argument, an interview, a match.
